response: Yes, 263 is prime. It is not divisible by any prime less than or equal to its square root (≈16.2), such as 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, or 13.
reasoning_content: We need to check if 263 is prime. Let's test divisibility by small primes: 2 (no), 3 (sum digits 2+6+3=11, not divisible by 3), 5 (no), 7: 7*37=259, remainder 4, so not divisible. 11: 11*23=253, remainder 10; 11*24=264, too high, so no. 13: 13*20=260, remainder 3; 13*21=273, too high. 17: 17*15=255, remainder 8; 17*16=272, too high. 19: 19*13=247, remainder 16; 19*14=266, too high. 23: 23*11=253, remainder 10; 23*12=276, too high. 29: 29*9=261, remainder 2; 29*10=290, too high. 31: 31*8=248, remainder 15; 31*9=279, too high. 37: 37*7=259, remainder 4; 37*8=296, too high. 41: 41*6=246, remainder 17; 41*7=287, too high. 43: 43*6=258, remainder 5; 43*7=301. 47: 47*5=235, remainder 28; 47*6=282. 53: 53*4=212, remainder 51; 53*5=265, too high. Next prime sqrt(263) is about 16.2, so we only need to test primes up to 13? Actually sqrt(263) ≈ 16.22, so primes up to 13 (since 17 > 16.22). We already tested 2,3,5,7,11,13. None divide. So 263 is prime. Quick sanity check: Yes, it's prime.
